title: Why Do Some People Get Chills From Music and Others Don't? — Daniel Fox

schema.headline: Why Do Some People Get Chills From Music and Others Don't?
schema.description: Why do some people get chills from music and other people feel nothing? You know the moment — a song hits a certain point and a shiver runs up the back of your neck, your arms prickle, something in your chest goes tight for a second.
about: Why Do Some People Get Chills From Music

faq.q: So why do some people get chills and others feel nothing?
faq.a: Two parts. The *what*: Blood & Zatorre (2001) scanned people listening to the song that reliably gives them chills and watched the reward system fire — ventral striatum, midbrain, orbitofrontal cortex, the same circuitry as food, sex, and drugs — while fear-and-unease regions like the amygdala quieted down. The *why-you*: Nusbaum & Silvia (2011) ran people through the Big Five and found only one trait predicts chills — openness to experience. Not agreeableness, not how much you happen to like music. Openness.

faq.q: Does it mean I love music less if I never get chills?
faq.a: No. The chill isn't a measure of how much you enjoy music — it's a measure of how deep you go into it. Think of a song as a building with a lot of rooms. Most people stand in the lobby; high-openness listeners walk the halls and track the moment the music sets something up and pays it off. That payoff is what feeds the reward system. It's mostly attention, not a fixed setting.

The brain-scan side is grade-A, well-replicated neuroscience. The personality side is real but lighter — a single good study on a modest sample, self-reported, correlational, explaining a slice of the difference between people, not all of it. Evidence grade for this one: B. Full citations in the description.
